coredata.dc:title: Facile approach to synthesize highly fluorescent multicolor emissive carbon dots via surface functionalization for cellular imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Kundu, Aniruddha
Lee, Jungpyo
Park, Byeongho
Ray, Chaiti
Sankar, K. Vijaya
Kim, Wook Sung
Lee, Soo Hyun
Cho, Il-Joo
Jun, Seong Chan
coredata.dc:description: Luminescent nanomaterials are encouraging scaffolds for diverse applications such as chemical sensors and biosensors, imaging, drug delivery, diagnostics, catalysis, energy, photonics, medicine, and so on. Carbon dots (CDs) are a new class of luminescent carbonaceous nanomaterial that have appeared recently and reaped tremendous scientific interest. Herein, we have exploited a simple approach to prepare tuneable and highly fluorescent CDs via surface functionalization. The successful synthesis of CDs is manifested from several investigations like high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The CDs exhibit excellent water solubility and with increasing nitrogen content fluorescence quantum yield increases whereas cell toxicity decreases. The CD synthesized at high temperature (180 °C) shows very high quantum yield (more than 56%). The tuneable optical properties of CDs are systematically studied using UV-vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The cell viability evaluation and in vitro imaging study reveals that the synthesized CDs can be employed as a potential fluorescent probe for bio-imaging without further modification.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Surface functionalization
Fluorescence
Multicolor emission
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthetic strategy and characterization of CDs
Cytotoxicity evaluation and cellular imaging of CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was partially supported by the Priority Research Centers Program ( 2009-0093823 ), and the Korean Government (MSIP) (No. 2015R1A5A1037668 ), Brain Korea 21 plus (BK21) project grant funded by Korea government, Korea Ministry of Environment as “Global Top Project” ( 2016002130005 ), and Development of diagnostic system for mild cognitive impairment due to Alzheimer's disease ( 2015-11-1684 ), Nano·Material Technology Development Program ( NRF-2017M3A7B4041987 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the synthesis of surface functionalized CDs and its application for in vitro cellular imaging.
(a, b) TEM images with different resolution, (c) Size histogram, (d) HRTEM image, (e) selected area FFT image of CD-H and (f) XRD pattern for CD synthesized at different temperature.
(a) FTIR spectra and (b) Survey scans XPS for different CDs, (c, d) high resolution deconvulated C1s and O1s spectra for CD-H.

(a–c) UV-Vis absorption (black line), excitation (blue line) and emission (green line) spectra and (d–f) excitation wavelength dependent emission spectra for CD-L, CD-M, CD-H respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
(a) Bright field image and Fluorescence images of CD-H at different excitation wavelengths (b) 405, (c) 488 and (d) 555 nm.
Bright field and fluorescent images of U87 MG cells incubated with CD-H (a–d), CD-M (e–h) and imaged under 405 (b, f), 488 nm (c, g) excitation and merged images (d, h). [Scale bar is 50 µm].
Bright field and fluorescent images of 3T3 cells incubated with CD-H (a–d), CD-M (e–h) and imaged under 405 (b, f), 488 nm (c, g) excitation and merged images (d, h). [Scale bar is 50 µm].
Comparison of several parameters for the synthesis and application of CD.

row.entry: Poly(sodium4-styrene sulfonate) and Poly(4-styrene sulfonic acid co-maleic acid)
Imaging of mycobacterium and fungal cells
Imaging of human brain tumor, fibroblast, neuron and astrocyte cells

head.ce:title: Facile approach to synthesize highly fluorescent multicolor emissive carbon dots via surface functionalization for cellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical Engineering, Yonsei University, Seoul 120-749, South Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical Engineering, Pohang University of Science and Technology (POSTECH), Pohang 790-784, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Pohang University of Science and Technology (POSTECH)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for BioMicrosystems, Brain Science Institute, Korea Institute of Science and Technology, Seoul 136-791, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Korea Institute of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Luminescent nanomaterials are encouraging scaffolds for diverse applications such as chemical sensors and biosensors, imaging, drug delivery, diagnostics, catalysis, energy, photonics, medicine, and so on. Carbon dots (CDs) are a new class of luminescent carbonaceous nanomaterial that have appeared recently and reaped tremendous scientific interest. Herein, we have exploited a simple approach to prepare tuneable and highly fluorescent CDs via surface functionalization. The successful synthesis of CDs is manifested from several investigations like high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The CDs exhibit excellent water solubility and with increasing nitrogen content fluorescence quantum yield increases whereas cell toxicity decreases. The CD synthesized at high temperature (180 °C) shows very high quantum yield (more than 56%). The tuneable optical properties of CDs are systematically studied using UV-vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The cell viability evaluation and in vitro imaging study reveals that the synthesized CDs can be employed as a potential fluorescent probe for bio-imaging without further modification.

ce:section.ce:para: A large number of new nanostructured carbon-based materials such as graphene, fullerenes, nano-diamonds, carbon nanotubes etc. have been discovered during the past decades, which exhibit unique physicochemical properties [1]. Recently, a new type of carbonaceous nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) also known as carbon nanodots (CNDs) or carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted stubborn scientific attention, since its serendipitous discovery during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) through electrophoresis by Scrivens and co-workers in 2004 [2]. As a new rising star in the field of luminescent carbon nanomaterials, CDs have harvested much interest as potential candidates to conventional semiconductor QDs. Though they exhibit comparable optical properties, CDs have several advantages over QDs such as low toxicity, excellent water dispersibility, environmental friendliness, high photo-stability and chemical inertness [3–5]. Moreover, simple and inexpensive one step synthesis along with either surface passivation or functionalization of CDs permits for the regulation of their various properties. For that reason, CDs find versatile applications in biological imaging, drug delivery, sensing of metal ions, optoelectronic devices, catalysis, solar cells and so on [6–11]. Recently, our group has studied the optical properties of graphene/functionalized graphene and graphene quantum dots which mainly focused on their luminescent behavior [12–16].

In the past few years, various physico-chemical methods have been explored to prepare CDs, including laser ablation of graphite, arc discharge, electrochemical synthesis, oxidative acid treatment, microwave/ultrasonic methods, thermal oxidation etc [2,17–22]. But, these aforementioned methods involve the use of strong acid, expensive starting materials, sophisticated instruments and complicated processes which limit the applications of synthesized materials. Numerous carbon based materials like graphite, SWCNTs/MWCNTs, candle soot, glycerol, carbohydrate, citrate, polymers are used as starting materials for the synthesis of CDs [23–30]. Thus, for large-scale synthesis of high quality fluorescent CDs simple, environment friendly and sustainable synthetic routes are of potential interest and the hydrothermal method has been exposed to be a facile and effective synthetic approach to produce fluorescent CDs [31]. Recently, polymers are used as surface passivating agent to obtain multi-responsive fluorescence property. For example, hyperbranched polyethyleneimine (PEI) capped fluorescent Ag nanoclusters/CDs are prepared and employed for different sensing phenomenon [32–34]. In this article, branched polyethyleneimine (PEI) functionalized CDs are synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal assisted pyrolysis of citric acid (CA). Here, the formation of CDs and the surface passivation with PEI are achieved simultaneously, utilizing this one pot strategy. We have also thoroughly investigated the effect of temperature and reaction time on the fluorescence property of CDs. The obtained CDs show excellent fluorescence properties and excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior, which will really be helpful for multicolor imaging. Moreover, the CDs synthesized at high temperature exhibit better quantum yield and low cytotoxicity even at high concentration, compared to the CDs obtain at lower temperature. These advantages inspired us to employ as synthesized highly fluorescent and stable CDs as an excellent fluorescent probe for in vitro cellular imaging.

Branched polyethyleneimine (PEI, Mw = 1200) and citric acid (CA, ACS reagent) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Syringe filter (pore size 0.1 µm) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. All other chemicals used in the cellular experiments were purchased from Gibco, USA and Life Technologies, USA. Deionized (DI) water was used throughout the experiments.

The fluorescent CDs passivated with branched PEI was synthesized by a facile green route of hydrothermal assisted pyrolysis method. For that purpose, at first equimolar mixture of CA and PEI was dissolved in DI water using different glass vial. Next, these two solutions were mixed and sonicated for 5 min to get homogeneous mixture and transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave chamber. The sealed autoclave was heated with variable temperature (100, 150 and 180 °C) and time was also varied (5, 10, 15 and 20 h) to obtain a vivid idea about the formation of CDs. After the execution of hydrothermal reaction, the clear viscous liquid turned into either pale yellow or bright yellow colour solution depending on the reaction condition, suggesting the formation of carbon dots. The resulted solution was further filtered using a syringe filter to discard large-sized carbon nanoparticles and stored at ambient condition for future study. The CDs synthesized at different temperature are denoted as CD-L (100 °C), CD-M (150 °C) and CD-H (180 °C).

The relative fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CD was measured using quinine sulphate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (quantum yield 54%) as a standard [35]. The value of QY was calculated according to the following equation:
QY
sample
=
QY
std .
( I/A )
sample
×
( A/I )
std .
η
sample
2
/
η
std .
2
where ‘A’ denotes absorbance at the excitation wavelength and ‘η’ is the refractive index of solvent. The integrated emission intensity is represented by ‘I’, which is calculated from the area under the emission peak on the same wavelength scale. To minimize the re-absorption effects at the excitation wavelength, the absorbance values of both sample and standard solutions were kept below 0.1.

Here, we have used different cell lines to check the in vitro cell viability assay and cell imaging. For instance, human brain tumor cells (U87 MG), standard fibroblast cell (3T3), and two primary cells (neuron and astrocyte) are used. Astrocytes were isolated from postnatal day 1 Sprague-Dawley (SD) rats. On the other hand, the primary rat cortical neurons were isolated from embryonic day 18 Sprague-Dawley (SD) rats. Using neural tissue dissociation KIT (MACS, Miltenyi Biotec, USA), neurons and astrocytes were dissociated.

Isolated neurons and astrocytes were seeded respectively into a 24-well culture plate which involves poly d-lysine (PDL) coated round type coverslips at 1.0 × 105 cell per well in Neurobasal cell culture medium (NBM; Gibco, USA) with 2% B27 supplement (Life Technologies, USA), 0.25% Glutamax (Gibco, USA), and 1% penicillin-streptomycin (Gibco, USA). The samples were cultured for 72 h at 37 °C in a humidified 5% CO2 incubator. Then, neurons and astrocytes were incubated with 100 μg/mL CDs in NBM medium for 2 h. After 3 times washing with phosphate buffered saline (PBS), neurons and astrocyte were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 30 min at room temperature. Finally, cellular fluorescence images were taken by confocal laser scanning microscope (Zeiss LSM700, Carl Zeiss, Germany) after washing with PBS.

U87 MG and 3T3 cells were seeded respectively into a 24-well culture plate at 1.0 × 105 cells per well in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) mixed with 10% of fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin-streptomycin. The cells were cultured for 24 h at 37 °C in a humidified 5% CO2 incubator. Then, the U87 MG and 3T3 cells were incubated with 100 μg/mL CDs in DMEM medium for 2 h. Then the same trend was followed to image the cells as described above.

The cytotoxicity of CD was evaluated by WST (2-(2-methoxy-4-nitrophenyl)-3-(4-nitrophenyl)-5-(2,4-disulfophenyl)-2H-tetrazolium) assay using Ez-Cytox Cell Viability assay kit against different cells. For U87 MG and 3T3 cells, firstly, 8 × 103 cells per well were seeded into 96-well culture plates with 100 μL cell culture medium containing DMEM with 10% FBS, 1% penicillin-streptomycin and incubated for 12 h at 37 °C in a humidified 5% CO2 incubator. After overnight growth, CD solution with various concentrations (10, 20, 50, 100, 200, and 400 μg/mL) was added into each well and incubated for another 48 h at 37 °C. The cell culture medium was replaced with 100 μL of fresh medium and then 10 μL of the WST solution was added to each well and further incubated for 4 h. Then, after transferring 100 μL supernatant into new 96-well culture plates the absorbance was measured at 450 nm. The relative cell viability was calculated assuming 100% cell viability for cells without any CDs.

HRTEM analysis was performed in JEM-ARM200F atomic resolution analytical microscope. UV-Vis absorption features of aqueous solutions of the samples were recorded with a UV-Vis spectrophotometer (JASCO Corporation, V-650). The fluorescence characteristics of the samples were studied in a fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, LS-55) with a He-Cd laser source and using a quartz cuvette of 1 cm path length. The characteristics of the samples were analysed using an X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS) (Thermo UK, K-alpha) with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source, an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Smart Lab) with a 9 kW rotating anode X-ray source. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was studied on a Perkin Elmer Spectrum 100 FTIR spectrometer by drop casting the CD solution on silicon wafer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthetic strategy and characterization of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: A simple, one-step and environmentally benign hydrothermal assisted pyrolysis route was used for the synthesis of water soluble, fluorescent CDs (shown in Fig. 1 ), where CA was used as carbon source and branched PEI acted as surface passivation/N-doping agent.

The reaction temperature and hydrothermal time play a vital role to obtain CDs with enhanced physical (stability, fluorescence etc.) properties. For that purpose to optimize the reaction condition we have varied all these parameters and also to get an idea about the surface passivation we have synthesized CDs without using PEI.

The morphology and structures of the CD synthesized at 180 °C (CD-H) were studied by TEM image and displayed in Fig. 2 . The statistical particle size distributions of the CDs clearly exhibit uniform dispersion and a relatively narrow size distribution ranging from 2 to 5 nm with an average diameter of 3 nm (Fig. 2c). Again, the HRTEM image (Fig. 2d) and the corresponding area of the fast Fourier transform image (Fig. 2e) show noticeable fringe pattern. The lattice fringe spacing is calculated to be 0.21 nm indicating that the crystalline core of CD is graphitic in nature. The absence of prominent lattice fringe is due to the wrapping of crystalline part by non-crystalline region of PEI chains [36]. Moreover, in the XRD (Fig. 2f) patterns; the CDs show two peaks at 18.6°–21° and 41°, respectively. These two peaks can be assigned to the characteristic peaks for amorphous and crystalline part. Here, the major graphitic diffraction peaks of CD are undistinguishable which may be affected by the broad peaks, further demonstrating that a large amount of PEI chains covers the graphitizing core [36]. Thus, it can be concluded that the CD is composed of both graphite core and a non-graphitic surface that consists of PEI chains and different functional groups.

To gain structural insight about the surface states (presence of functional groups on the surface) and compositions of the CDs, FTIR and XPS spectroscopy were further employed. Fig. 3 a represents the FTIR spectra of CD synthesized at different temperature and confirm the presence of functional groups such as OH/NH in the region of 3280 cm−1. The absorption around 2820 and 2930 cm−1 represent CH antisymmetric and symmetric stretching which comes from the backbone of PEI as well as the CDs. A strong peak at 1355 cm−1 appears due to the CN stretching of PEI. The NH in-plane bending of the secondary amine at 1559 cm−1 and the CN stretching of the primary amine around 1112 cm−1 provide significant information about the in situ grafting of PEI molecules onto the surface of CDs. Again, the peaks at 1657 cm−1 clearly depicts the successful surface functionalization, since this peak corresponds to the stretching vibrations of CO band (amide I band), which can be ascribed to the typical vibration of grafted OCNH functionalities. Furthermore, the signal at 1260 cm−1 is related to the different modes of COC or CO stretching presents in the CDs. The FTIR spectra of CD synthesized at 100 and 150 °C (CD-L and CD-M) clearly shows that at low temperature relatively lower surface functionalization occurs but at high temperature it improves. XPS findings were used to further investigate the surfaces of these samples and the full scan spectra for different CDs are depicted in Fig. 3b. The full spectra exhibit three typical peaks: C1s (285 eV), N1s (400 eV), and O1s (531 eV) which indicate that all the samples consisted of the same elements. In the high-resolution spectra, the C1s band can be deconvulated into three peaks (Fig. 3c and S1a,b), corresponding to sp2 carbons (CC, 284.5 eV), sp3 carbons (CO/CN, 286.1 eV) and carbonyl carbons (CO, 287.8 eV). The high resolution O1s spectra for different CDs are demonstrated in Fig. 3d and S1c,d; which is deconvulated into two peaks at 531.6 and at 533.0 eV for CO and CO, respectively. The XPS intensity at 531.6 eV dramatically increases from CD-L to CD-H, implying that the surface of the samples is very similar but the content of CO groups (due to formation of amide bonds) increases in CD-H due to improved surface functionalization. Moreover from the full scan spectra and Table 1 it is clear that the content of nitrogenous group or N-doping increases for CD-H. This phenomenon further supports that better degree of surface functionalization is achieved at higher temperature. Thus, it can be predicted that the embedded PEI molecules provide surface passivation, which gives CDs some advantages like, water solubility, water stability and strong fluorescence, which will be described below.

The optical properties of the CDs were characterized by UV-vis and fluorescence spectroscopic studies. The synthesized aqueous solution of CDs exhibit two absorption bands at 257 and 358 nm respectively (Fig. 4 a–c), which are attributed to π-π∗ transition from carbon core and n-π∗ transition from the surface [34]. Again, the appearance of high energy tail in the visible region of the absorption spectrum of the CDs is assigned to Mie scattering, which is probably a consequence of different nanosized particles and this phenomenon has become pronounced at the long wavelength absorbance [37]. The synthesized CDs show same absorption spectra but the excitation spectra are different, which is obvious from Figs. 4(a–c) and S2. The excitation spectra (Fig. S2) clearly depicts that CD-L exhibit two peaks near 325 and 424 nm. These two peaks can be ascribed due to transition from the σ and π orbital (HOMO) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) [38]. On the other hand, CD-M (438 nm) and CD-H (450 nm) show only one peak which indicate one kind of electronic transition. Although the fluorescence mechanisms for CDs still remain a debatable subject, the fascinating fluorescence properties with excitation dependent emission intensity, similar to semiconductor QDs continues to stimulate scientists to conduct deeper explorations [39–41]. Recent reports provide evidences that emission arises from lot of factors such as quantum effect, emissive traps and radiative recombination of excitons located at surface energy traps but more research is needed in this regard to get clear insight about the fluorescence of CDs [42,43]. Full width at half maxima (FWHM) of CD-L, CD-M and CD-H under the same excitation wavelength is calculated to be 106, 94 and 91 nm, respectively, indicating a narrow size distribution of CDs. Now, to obtain clear picture about the wavelength dependence feature of synthesized CDs the fluorescence spectra of CD solution were recorded by changing the excitation wavelengths. The excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence spectra of CD solution (1 mg/mL) are shown in Fig. 4(d–f), which clearly depicts that with changes in excitation wavelength, the emission peak position is red-shifted for all the CDs with a decrease of fluorescence intensity. The red shift of emission spectra indicates a strong dependence on the excitation wavelengths. The origin of excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence in CDs are also not very clear; we assume that this phenomenon may arise from the different distribution of emissive energy traps on the surface of CD. It has been argued that both surface state and size of CDs may play a vital role in determining the emission spectra of polymer passivated CDs [44]. Furthermore, from the results of XPS and FTIR, it is obvious that CDs contain different functional groups like CO, COC and CN on the surface which could result in a series of emissive traps between π and π∗ states of CC or CO and thus with change in excitation wavelength, different corresponding surface emissive trap becomes dominant resulting in the excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior [45,46]. Again, it is interesting to note that under the same excitation wavelength, CD-H shows a red shift of 30 nm compared to CD-L (Fig. S3) which is also demonstrated in Scheme S1. Here, we hypothesize that since, the CD surfaces are mainly composed of different functional groups (containing bonded nitrogen and oxygen atoms to the surface), and the bandgap of such a structure strongly depends on the incorporated nitrogen species. Now, with increasing number of nitrogen species (Table 1) the bandgap is reduced and hence red shift occurs. To optimize the hydrothermal condition we have performed supplementary experiments by altering the hydrothermal time and keeping the temperature fixed at 180 °C. The emission spectra of CD-H synthesized using different time are furnished in Fig. S4, which clearly demonstrates that CD-H prepared at 15 h renders better fluorescence property compared to others and thus we will select CD-H15 for further application. Furthermore, to examine the role of PEI on fluorescence property we have also synthesized CDs without using PEI. CDs obtained using only CA shows very poor fluorescence property as evidenced from Fig. S5. The relative fluorescence QY of the synthesized CDs were calculated using quinine sulphate as standard and summarized in Table-S1. The high QY of the synthesized CDs signifies that the incorporation of PEI renders better surface passivation to the CDs, since without passivation we could have ended with very weak emission [44]. Interestingly, the QYs increases with increase in nitrogen content (Tables 1 and S1), which may be ascribed to the favoured oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) on the surface of CDs [47,48]. As a consequence, the surface passivation is improved and hence augmentation in fluorescence intensity. The CD-H shows very high quantum yield value (56.23%), which is much higher compared to the recently reported values [49–54]. Again, to check the photo-stability of our synthesized CDs we have performed fluorescence measurement with different time interval after irradiation with 360 nm light and also captured optical images for CDs after illumination with 360 nm light (Fig. S6). It is obvious from the figure that even after 7 days CDs show very negligible photobleaching property. So, we can claim that our CDs can be stored for long term study and hence, useful for both optics as well as biological study. Thus, it can be concluded that in this hybrid CD, the grafted PEI displays two key roles: (i) acts as a surface passivation agent (nitrogen rich compound) to deliver CDs with strong fluorescence, since the surface defects may become more stable to facilitate radiative recombination of surface-confined electrons and holes, (ii) forms a protecting layer which endows CDs excellent water solubility. But, it is difficult to distinguish between the specific roles of surface passivation and N-doping, as the surface passivation and N-doping are achieved simultaneously. Moreover, CDs emit different coloured bright fluorescence including blue 1 For interpretation of color in Fig. 5, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.
1 , green and red at different excitation wavelengths in the solid state as depicted in Figs. 5 and S7,8. From the figures it is obvious that CD synthesized at high temperature shows better emissive properties which might be very useful in multicolour imaging applications. Furthermore, to define the novelty of our prescribed method we have made a comparison with other synthesized CDs available in literature and provided in Table 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity evaluation and cellular imaging of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Recently CDs are successfully utilized for cell imaging of different kind of cells which proves that CDs can be used for in vitro imaging [55–59]. In the present scenario, the facile synthetic procedure, high aqueous stability and enhanced fluorescence of the CDs inspired us to explore their potential applications in bioimaging. To evaluate the prospects of our CDs as a bioimaging material, different cell lines were used to estimate the in vitro cytotoxicity of the CDs via conventional WST assay. Hence, before using CDs for cell imaging, cytotoxicity study was carried out, since cytotoxicity is an indispensable factor in biomedical applications.

As shown in Fig. S9a, the WST assays of cell viability studies suggest that the CD-H reveals low cytotoxicity and are not found to pose any significant toxic effects even at a high concentration of 400 μg/mL whereas CD-L and CD-M exhibit high cytotoxicity against U87 MG cells. Again, CD-H shows good cell viability against fibroblast cells (3T3) up to 100 μg/mL which is adequate for biological applications and CD-M imposes little cytotoxicity (Fig. S9b). From these results it can be inferred that CDs synthesized at high temperature can be used for imaging or other biomedical applications. Hence, CD-H with low cytotoxicity was subjected to cellular imaging and in vitro cellular uptake experiments were performed using U87 MG and 3T3 cells. From, Fig. 6 a it is clear that cells keeps their normal morphology which suggests good biocompatibility of the synthesized CD-H. Again, the fluorescence imaging study (Fig. 6b, c) shows that significant intra-cellular blue and green fluorescence is observed by exciting at the wavelength of 405, 488 nm after incubation for 24 h with the CD-H. Moreover, it is also obvious from the overlay image (Fig. 6d) that fluorescent signals are not only distributed in the cytoplasm but the CDs are also internalized into the nucleus of cells. The uptake of these CDs by the cells could be similar to the pathway for graphene quantum dots, which penetrate into the cell membrane by disturbing the structure and conformation of the lipid bilayer [60,61]. The in vitro bioimaging ability for CD-M is also depicted in Fig. 6(e–h) which also renders same result like CD-H. Again, CD-H and CD-M has also been tested for cell imaging ability against 3T3 cells and presented in Fig. 7 , which clearly delivers same result to that of U87MG cells. Furthermore, the in vitro cellular imaging studies are also carried out for astrocyte and neuron cells using CD-H, CD-M (Fig. S10, S11). Here, we do not claim that our synthesized CD show any specific binding to particular cell structure, instead it is a general study for in vitro imaging of different kind of cells. In contrast to most unmodified CDs, here we believe that our synthesized CDs penetrate into the nucleus without any further modification, which is beneficial for the simplification of production procedure. Hence, from the above results it is obvious that both CD-H and CD-M possess the in vitro cell imaging ability for different kind of cells. But, in view of cytotoxicity study it is clear that CD-H is more suitable as a potential bioimaging probe for real-time applications, such as labelling of cell nucleus.

In summary, we have demonstrated a facile, green and economic method to synthesize surface functionalized carbon dots (CDs) using citric acid as carbon source and branched polyethylene imine as surface passivating/N-doping agent. The key step of preparation is the hydrothermal treatment, which is also beneficial for exploring the luminescence mechanisms of CDs because the samples differ only with regard to their surface states. The CDs synthesized at higher temperature exhibit better fluorescence property due to improved surface functionalization/N-doping and also depict good cell viability compared to CDs obtained at low temperature. Hence, we have studied the optical properties in depth to get clear idea about the luminescence of CDs. These CDs are successfully employed as a potential bioimaging probe for in vitro cellular imaging of U87 MG, 3T3, astrocyte and neuron cells. Interestingly, the CDs show good penetrability to nucleus and thus, these synthesized CDs can be used for targeted drug delivery without further functionalization. We believe these findings will pave a new way to extend the use of CDs for optical or biological applications in near future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partially supported by the Priority Research Centers Program (2009-0093823), and the Korean Government (MSIP) (No. 2015R1A5A1037668), Brain Korea 21 plus (BK21) project grant funded by Korea government, Korea Ministry of Environment as “Global Top Project” (2016002130005), and Development of diagnostic system for mild cognitive impairment due to Alzheimer's disease (2015-11-1684), Nano·Material Technology Development Program (NRF-2017M3A7B4041987).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: XPS spectra for CD-L, CD-M; Additional PL and PLE spectra; Table for quantum yield values; Fluorescence images for CD-L, CD-M; Cell viability for CDs using U87MG, 3T3 cells; Cell imaging for astrocyte and neuron cells. Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2017.10.095.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon nanomaterials for electronics, optoelectronics, photovoltaics, and sensing
Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications
Carbon quantum dots for optical bioimaging
Carbon nanomaterials for biological imaging and nanomedicinal therapy
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles for the fluorescent detection of metal ions
Carbon and graphene quantum dots for optoelectronic and energy devices: a review
Water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots and photocatalyst design
A fluorescent quenching performance enhancing principle for carbon nanodot-sensitized aqueous solar cells
Epoxy to carbonyl group conversion in graphene oxide thin films: effect on structural and luminescent characteristics
Controlling the luminescence emission from palladium grafted graphene oxide thin films via reduction
Tunable wide blue photoluminescence with europium decorated graphene
Surface plasmon enhancement of photoluminescence in photo-chemically synthesized graphene quantum dot and Au nanosphere
Reduced graphene oxide enwrapped phosphors for long-term thermally stable phosphor converted white light emitting diodes
One-step synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles by laser irradiation
Electrochemiluminescence of water-soluble carbon nanocrystals released electrochemically from graphite
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles: synthesis characterization, and bioimaging application
Microwave synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles with electrochemiluminescence properties
One-step ultrasonic synthesis of water-soluble carbon nanoparticles with excellent photoluminescent properties
Carbon-based dots co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur for high quantum yield and excitation-independent emission
In vivo NIR fluorescence imaging biodistribution, and toxicology of photoluminescent carbon dots produced from carbon nanotubes and graphite
An electrochemical avenue to blue luminescent nanocrystals from multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs)
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles derived from candle soot
One-step synthesis of surface passivated carbon nanodots by microwave assisted pyrolysis for enhanced multicolor photoluminescence and bioimaging
Simple aqueous solution route to luminescent carbogenic dots from carbohydrates
